CURRENT AFFAIRS.

The ** Trish Times’’ is badly off for something to
fling at Ireland when it trots ouf on its leader page a
-copy of a placard that was recently posted in Wexford.
1t heads the stuff *‘ Seditious Placards in Wexford.”
One might expect from the heading that Carson and
Co. had done a little bill-sticking down there. And
how does anyone know but that it was some Orangemen
that had these bills stuck up.  Though the “ Irish
Times "’ grabs at it and gives it great publicity on its
leader page, it is not worth much notice; and who can

el but that it may be part of the work of the Carrion
~ Crow flock. ; s

William Moote, K.C., M.P., made a speech breath-
ing rifles and last ditches to—the women of a branch
of the North Antrim Unionist Association. How the
old dames must have been thrilled when the toy revolu-
tionist declaved : —

It was in pursuance.and in furtherance of this
conspiracy to-keep the Union Jack flying, to pro-
tect themselves from Roman Catholic tyranny and
Nationalist -oppression that their clubs were still
drilling openly under the eyes of the police, prac-
tising route marching and perfecting themselves
in strength against the evil day when they would
be summoned to place their necks under the
Nationalist heel. It was in pursuance of this
conspiracy to assert the sovereign rights of the
United Kingdom that their rifle clubs were spring-
ing up all over Ulster, and their women folls were
being taught in first-aid classes.

Poor old Moore and poor old women!

A reader of ours some time back sent a letter to the
Department and signed his name in Irish. He got no
reply. Perhaps the Department had other reasons for
not replying beyond the fact that the letter was signed
in Irish. Later on the same gentleman wrote for a
copy of one of the leaflets and signed his name in Irish.
He is still without the leaflet, so we may take it that
it is his Irish signature that stands in the way. Now
if the Department is out to ignore Irish signatures it
would be well to turn the hose on it, if we may pub it
that way. Most of our readers will remember the
assault on Riverstown Post Office in Co. Sligo when
the Gaels bombarded the postmaster of that historic
village—the assault on Port Riverstown is one of the
outstanding incidents of the Irish Ireland campaign.
Why not turn a volley of Irish signed postcards on the
Department, and let_us see how the understrappers
who may be ignoring Irish signatures will behave under
the fire. The ammunition is cheap, for the letters or
postcards applying for leaflets need not be stamped.
So fire away. A few hundred shots ought to be enough
to bring the West Briton, or West Britons, concerned
to a proper sense of the realities of the situation. If
in spite of the attack the Department still flaunts the
anti-Irish flag, we can hold a council of war and decide
on further plans. But a hundred shots ought to bring

the, anti-Irish m}ders?mppers down.

There is a Bedstead Manufacturers’ Federation in
England, and it is putting up the price on the public.
As, so far as we know, there is no bedstead malker in
Ireland, Ireland has to pay more for its bedsteads
whilst no Irish workers get any benefit. We are in-
“formed that the bedstead trade in Ireland is increasing
year by year in consequence of the Irish farmers clear-
ing out the old wooden beds and having them replaced
by iron or metal. Why not some of our foundries in
the south go into the bedstead manufacture. There
is a fine field open for the industry in Treland, and
when a man like Mr. Governey was able to success-
fully establish such a highly complex industry as
modern boot-making in Carlow, we don’t see why the
bedstead industry, which is a much simpler class of
enterprise, could not be successfully established in
connection with some existing foundry.

A couple of weeks ago we eommented on the fact
that some dirty fluid was thrown upon people returning
from a Nationalist meeting in Mountmellick.  The
fluid was thrown from one of the windows, of a Protes-
tant business house in the town, Messrs. Flumphty,
Smyth and Sons. We now see by some editorial notes
in a local paper that the explanation, which satisfies
our local confemporary, is that the matter was a
dental. The Town Commissioners' of Mountmellic
we read, are also perfectly satisfied with the explana-
tion. As we drew attention to the matter a few weelks
ago, it is only just to put the explanation also on
record.

THE FIRST NEW YORK FEIS.

HE Gael has made blasé, sensation-sated New
York ‘“sit up and take notice,”” and this
happened at the first Feis which the Gaelic League has
organised in this city. Donal O’Connor, the organiser
who lately came to America to act in co-operation with
the Gaelic League delegation, arrived in this city a
short time ago, and when he told his intention of hold-
ing a I'eis here a lot of us thought he would not be able
to do it. He, however, gathered around him what we
may call our own trusty little band of workers
after a lot of work, on Sunday, May 19th, Celtic Park
the place where the Irish-American athletes train, was
converted into a very close copy of the Feis grounds
we all knew so well in Ireland. There was the big
field, grateful and green in the May sunshine, and a
platform had been erected at one end of the field for
the dancing and other competitions.

JUNE 8, 1912.

The word Feis, which was a very strange one to most
of the Trish here, was an advertisement in itself, and
the tall of competitions in singing, dancing, story-
telling, etc., coupled with the efforts of an exceedingly
energetic committee, had everyone who was at all inter-
ested in Irish affairs well posted on the fact that the
L'eis (or ““ fish,”” as some of them called it), was to be
something entirely new.

A football match between teams representing Kerry
and Kildare was also a magnet to get the crowd to
Celtic Park, and when matters got well under way by
3 p.m. there were about 6,000 people on the grounds;
later comers swelled the attendance to about double
this number. . Now getting down to brass tacks, the
crowded stands, the piper and fiddler on the platform,
the expectant people who intended competing in the
various events, the National costumes on a number of
people, and the undefinable air of Irish Ireland brought
one back to, say, about ten years ago, and the kind of
Peis we used to have in such a place as Navan, Wex-
ford or Castlebellingham, and the heart in a fellow grew
big at the thought that now we were going to have a
real Irish day.

A bright gleam of colour was given to the affair by
the presence of a company of the Irish Volunteers in
their natty blue and white regimentals. They were a
picked company, under the command of Captain
O’Sullivan, of Brooklyn, and gave an exhibition of rifle
drill, marching, and the rest of the military *‘ stunts.”
When Padraig O'Daly, the Gaelic League Secretary, saw
them he threw his hands in the air and shouted, ** Holy
saints, where did you get the soldiers?’’ and later on
when one of these lads in blue rested his arms on his
rifie butt and gave Padraig a string of sweet County
Waterford Gaelic, our secretarial friend indulged in a
typical Roosveltian grin and ignored the shouted re-
quests of poor Donal O’Connor that he come over and
help judge the story-telling contest. By the way,
evervone here is remarking on the likeness Padiaig
bears to the ex-President of the United States.

Dancing (and fine dancing, too), piping, singing,
history, applause, shouts in Gaellc‘ and Enghsh,
announcements and speeches, it all mingled into one
arand day; and when Henry Flynn, President of the

N.Y, State Gaelic League, introduced ' Bishop
O’Dougherty, from the Philippines, you should have
heard them shout. The young bishop gave us a
straight-from-the-shoulder pronouncement of Irish
principles, all good common-sense and no oratorical
trimmings, and maybe we didn’t like it. Following
him we had O’Daly himself, and mind you, he has got
to be a good, witty speaker. He discussed his pet
topic, the National Board, and spoke so that he hit
right home. TFather O'Flanagan then stepped to the
front, and the Father has been doing so much speech-
making lately that he can get the crowd with him from
the start. In his speech he covered the situatior in a .
concise, taking manner; and as the majority of the
crowd there did not know quite as much about the
rx}n])vemenb as we would like them, his talk was valu-
able.

The fact that there were present a big majority of
Trish people who had but a very vague idea of what this
Gaelic revival is all about, and how it bears on the pros-
perity of the country, and that the matter was then
fully brought home to them, is one of the biggest bene-
fits the Feis brought us; and as the organiser and dele-
gates are projecting a series of them all over America,
we can look for good results from them. The number
of Trish people in this land is so great, and the conse-
quent loss to Ireland so big, that the least the Irish in
America owe the old land is sufficient funds to keep the
Gaelic League financially sound.

It is the intention to malke this New York Feis an
annual affair, and we can reasonably expect to improve
as we go on, so that we may in time develop quite a
creditable junior Oireachtas here, where the winners in
the various feisanna on this side of the water may dis-
play their prowess. And there are various other ideas
afloat which may materialise into something worth
while soon.

THE JACOB AFFAIR.

7 THE proposed development of Jacob's factory in’

Liverpool is very unsatisfactory reading for Ive-
land. Surely the practical difficully of extending their
present factory could be met by taking new land on
the outskirts of Dublin as well as by taking it .on the
outskirts of Liverpool. Jacob’s is a successful Irish
industry with a large export trade, and the only
apparent advantage that a site on the outskirts of Ljver-
pool would have over one in Dublin is the saving of
some carriage. And would that be much of a saving?
If it were the other way about and a Liverpool factory
had a big business in Ireland, the threugh rate carriage
from Liverpool to the interior of Treland as against the
rate from Dublin to the interior of Ireland would
searcely induce the Liverpool factory to open a manu-
facturing branch in Dublin.  Suppose that Jacob’s
start their manufacturing branch on the outskirts of
Liverpool, will not the tendency be to gradually drop
the manufacturing branch in Ireland, for the cost of
carriage from Liverpool to inland centres in Ireland
will not be much, if any, greater—perhaps it might be
less in some cases—than from Dublin. ~ The through
rates from Dublin to various centres in England and
the Continent cannot surely—certainly they ought not
—Dbe very much greater than the rates to these places
from a centre in England like the outskirls of Liver-
pool.  When it is a question of getting Fnglish stuff
into Ireland, the Irish manufacturers in competition
have, owing to the through rate, little or no advantage.
How can it be then that cost of carriage would induce
the shifting of manufacture from Dublin to Liverpool ?
Are the Irish nation- ailways at the bottom of it?
There is, of course, tI ying of some raw materials to
Dublin in the first instance, but as flour is the chief
vaw material concerned, why not get Irish milled flour
which can compete with English milled flour? As for
other things like currants and raisins, they have to be
imported to England,, so that we caunot see how the
carriage of them to Dublin rather than to Liverpo:)
can make much difference.

IRISH-GROWN FLOWERS.

HEN the Lraper, some four years ago, advo-
X cated the purchase by Irish buyers of Irish-
grown flowers, Irish-grown fruit, and Irish-grown
vegetables, in order that Trish workers shoulfl find
employment at home instead of having to emigrate,
success seemed very doubtful. But the LEADER, after
its usual policy, persevered, and now Irish-grown
flowers, fruit, and vegetables find a ready market,
which, however, should be much larger.

I was recently in a large and prosperous English
town (Bournemouth—population, 80,000), and I was
surprised to find that the English inhabitants had in
some way imbibed the LEADER’S views, and that Eng-
lish produce got a marked preference. In the flower-
shops, such of the tulips, lilies, carnations, stocks,
etc., as were English-grown were marked ‘‘ English-
grown,”” ‘“ Grown in our own gardens,”’ and so on.
Sometimes it was added that the flowers had been cut
that same morning.

So with the vegetables—'* English-grown aspara-
gus,”” ““ English-grown spinage,’”” and so on.

Honey was stated to be made in England. Even
the fish-sellers were proud to state on their labels that
the fish exhibited had been ‘‘ caught locally,’” or that
they had been taken ‘‘ off Christ’s Church,”” a noted
fishing place.

John Bull, in his own quiet plodding way, had
grasped the importance of encouraging local industries,
and without a meeting or a ‘‘ resolution,’”” had acted
on his belief. ~ John saw that, although the principle
of buying in the cheapest market was very well in its
way, the necessity of encouraging local workers was
also important, and that there was no necessary con-
tradiction between these two principles.

In the case of flowers, this was very remarkable.
The Channel Islands were almost at the door of Bourne-
mouth, steamers came every day from Guernsey and
Jersey to Southampton, yet flowers marked as Eng-
hsh-grown ﬂower's "" were exhibited in great numbers.

My wife went into a florist’s shop and had a conver-

as regards freshness and brightness and beauty, no
altar in the United Kingdom surpassed Rathgar on
Holy Thursday; and they were all Irish-grown flowers.

Other churches in the city also secure genuine Irish
flowers by placing their orders in good time. Recently,
too, thanks to the LEADER, country towns are, to some
extent, working up to the importance of encouraging
the cultivation of Irish flowers, and I have seen, with
unalloyed pleasure, orders for such flowers from the
counties Cavan, Limerick, and Wexford.

There are many things which we can do for our-
selves without any help from Government Boards. As
a matter of fact, we are too much disposed to place
ourselves in the hands of these Boards, instead of act-
ing independently. When buying flowers, or fruit, or
vegetables, we can always give a preference to Irish
flowers, Irish fruit, Irish vegetables; and if we do this,
and then take some little trouble to make sure that we
get the genuine article, we can—each of us in his own
way—play a part in the good work of finding employ-
ment for the unemployed.

More than a hundred Irish young people went down
with the ill-fated ‘‘ Titanic.”” As these were third-
class passengers, the presumption is that they were
going to America to earn a living by working. At the
very time that these young people embarked hundreds
and thousands of pounds of Irish money were also
going out of the country to give employment to
foreigners in producing articles which, by a little care,
could be produced ‘‘as good and as cheap ” at home.
When purchasing, we should always inquire for Trish-
made goods. If the seller is an honest man and has
such goods he will supply them; but we should use a
certain amount of vigilance to make sure that the seller
keeps to his contract. The boxes of foreign flowers
which T saw on the Holyhead-Kingstown steamer must
have got, eventually, into the hands of Irish buyers.

NHE English ‘‘ Pernicious Literature Committee "
printed its annual report last month. Although
the report is intended for private circulation only, per-
mission has been kindly given for the publication of
suggestions and facts contained in it As the struggle
with us is against exactly the same evils as the struggle
in Ingland, some facts connected with the English
crusade cannot fail to be interesting, as the suggestions
of the English workers cannot fail to be useful to Irish
workers.

These suggestions originated from a request of the
Salford Catholic Federation, who wrote to the Per-
nicious Literature Committee asking for hints as to the
working of a crusade. The Committee, although pre-
dominantly, if not exclusively, Protestant, welcomes
all worl in the cause, and sent in reply the follow-
ing very practical directions:— i

““ 1. That a competent person should be appointed as
secretary, to advise members and make use of the infor-,
mation they may bring him {o the best purpose. He will
need to be very enthusiastic and very discreet. Through
him we could keep in touch with your work and give
advice and help: and he could let us have any informa-
tion he might obtain about bad books or questionable
beols on which we could report. 3

““2. That the methods of working be as follows:—

““ Bad pictures and picture postcards.—When these
are scen displayed in a shop window or upon a stall,
let the observer note the exact location (as for instance,
3rd row from top, 4th card from left side of window,
and so on), and the name of the shop. This informa-
tion should be given to the Chief Constable or Superin-
tendent of Police for the district: he, if he thinks it
wise, can send a plain-clothes officer to examine and

purchase, and the next step will be a seizure and pro- -

secution by the authorities. 7

awakened.”’
